message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: The single highest-value, directly-actionable result in the literature: WebWeaver's ablation shows that switching from MONOLITHIC composition to HIERARCHICAL section-by-section writing — with retrieval held constant — moves insight 42.72 → 50.02 (+7.3) and readability 43.82 → 49.79 (+6.0). This is precisely POLARIS's failure mode and it is a writer-side fix, not a retrieval fix.
DETAIL: Quote: 'we conducted a critical ablation study comparing our hierarchical writer against a brute-force baseline that attempts to include the entire memory bank to generate the final report in multiple steps... The most striking improvements are in insight (42.72 → 50.02) and readability (43.82 → 49.79), which directly validates our hypothesis on attentional management; by focusing the model on a curated context for each section, it can perform deeper reasoning rather than shallow summarization.' The mechanism: for each outline section the writer (1) issues a targeted RETRIEVE of only that section's cited evidence from the memory bank, (2) runs an explicit THINK action ('analyzes the previous sections and retrieved content, synthesizes key insights, selects the most compelling pieces of evidence, and formulates a coherent narrative structure' — 'This internal monologue is critical for moving beyond simple summarization to genuine synthesis'), (3) emits prose in <write> tags, then (4) PRUNES that section's sources from context and replaces them with a placeholder before the next section. The prune step is what prevents 'contextual bleeding'. POLARIS writes 12 monolithic 633-word paragraphs — this is the brute-force baseline that scores 42.72 insight.
SYSTEM: other
SOURCE GIVEN: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Find the ablation study comparing the hierarchical writer against a brute-force / monolithic baseline. Report exact numbers for insight, readability, comprehensiveness, depth, and any other RACE dimensions. Also quote the exact sentences about the ablation. Also report what judge model / evaluator was used for DeepResearch Bench RACE scoring. Also describe the writer's retrieve/think/write/prune loop if present.

input.query: WebWeaver hierarchical writer ablation insight 42.72 50.02 DeepResearch Bench

content.content:
Web search results for query: "WebWeaver hierarchical writer ablation insight 42.72 50.02 DeepResearch Bench"

Links: [{"title":"WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence withDynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3"},{"title":"WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence with Dynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research | alphaXiv","url":"https://www.alphaxiv.org/overview/2509.13312v2"},{"title":"[Literature Review] WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence with Dynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://www.themoonlight.io/en/review/webweaver-structuring-web-scale-evidence-with-dynamic-outlines-for-open-ended-deep-research"},{"title":"WebWeaver — Open Source Framework for Deep Research Outperforms OpenAI DeepResearch and Gemini Deep Research on Benchmarks","url":"https://neurohive.io/en/frameworks/webweaver-open-source-framework-for-deep-research-outperforms-openai-deepresearch-and-gemini-deep-research-on-benchmarks/"},{"title":"(PDF) WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence with Dynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/395542591_WebWeaver_Structuring_Web-Scale_Evidence_with_Dynamic_Outlines_for_Open-Ended_Deep_Research"},{"title":"WEBWEAVER: STRUCTURING WEB-SCALE EVIDENCE","url":"https://openreview.net/pdf/77d8174c05fa83703b67e4ba5e65be0251e399b9.pdf"},{"title":"[2509.13312v1] WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence with Dynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.13312v1"},{"title":"WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence with Dynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.13312"},{"title":"WebWeaver: Dynamic Outlines for Deep Research","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2509.13312"},{"title":"DeepResearch/WebAgent/WebWeaver at main · Alibaba-NLP/DeepResearch","url":"https://github.com/Alibaba-NLP/DeepResearch/tree/main/WebAgent/WebWeaver"}]

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about WebWeaver's hierarchical writer ablation study.

The most striking improvements from WebWeaver's hierarchical writing approach are in insight (42.72 → 50.02) and readability (43.82 → 49.79), which directly validates the hypothesis on attentional management; by focusing the model on a curated context for each section, it can perform deeper reasoning rather than shallow summarization.

The ablation study compared WebWeaver's hierarchical writer against a brute-force baseline that attempts to include the entire memory bank to generate the final report in multiple steps, which is similar to the workflow of LongWriter. The hierarchical approach dramatically outperforms the brute-force method across every metric, confirming that a hierarchical writing strategy is essential.

Additionally, the memory-grounded hierarchical writing approach also showed significant improvements in citation accuracy (86.73 → 93.37%). These results were measured on the DeepResearch Bench benchmark and demonstrate the effectiveness of WebWeaver's hierarchical synthesis strategy for open-ended deep research tasks.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# WebWeaver Ablation Study: Hierarchical vs. Brute-Force Writing

## Exact Performance Numbers

According to Figures 10-11, the hierarchical writer dramatically outperforms the brute-force baseline:

**DeepResearch Bench (RACE metrics):**
- Insight: 42.72 → 50.02 (+7.30 points)
- Readability: 43.82 → 49.79 (+5.97 points)
- Comprehensiveness: Not explicitly shown in figures

**DeepResearchGym:**
- Depth: Substantial improvement shown visually
- Support/Citation Accuracy: 86.73 → 93.37 (+6.64 points)

## Exact Ablation Study Quote

"our hierarchical approach dramatically outperforms the brute-force method across every metric"

The comparison contrasted "our hierarchical writer against a brute-force baseline that attempts to include the entire memory bank to generate the final report in multiple steps"

## Judge Model for RACE Scoring

Per section 4.1 Setup: **Gemini-2.5-pro** was adopted as the judgement model for DeepResearch Bench RACE evaluation.

## Writer's Retrieve/Think/Write/Prune Loop

The writer executes a structured cycle per section:

1. **Retrieve**: "targeted retrieval action, pulling only the relevant evidence from the memory bank"
2. **Think**: Analyzes previous sections and retrieved content
3. **Write**: Composes prose within "<write>" tags
4. **Prune**: "source materials are explicitly pruned from the context window and replaced with a placeholder message"

This mechanism ensures "the writer's context remains highly relevant and coherent for the next cycle"

input.prompt: Quote verbatim: (1) the ablation paragraph about hierarchical writer vs brute-force baseline including the insight and readability numbers; (2) the description of the THINK action in the writer; (3) the setup section stating which judge model is used for DeepResearch Bench RACE. Also state whether retrieval/research stage was held constant in that ablation.

content.content:
I appreciate your request, but I'm unable to fulfill it. The webpage content provided only shows a verification/login page for OpenReview.net. It doesn't contain:

1. Any ablation study paragraph comparing hierarchical writer vs brute-force baseline
2. Any description of a THINK action in a writer
3. Any setup section about judge models for DeepResearch Bench RACE
4. Information about retrieval/research stage control in ablations

To obtain these specific technical details, you would need to access the full PDF paper on OpenReview (referenced in the redirect URL), which requires completing their browser verification or signing in. The verification page itself contains no substantive research content.

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"SWE-Bench Leaderboard May 2026 | GPT-5.5 Leads at 88.7%","url":"https://www.marc0.dev/en/leaderboard"},{"title":"GPT-5.6 Sol Benchmarks Deep Dive | Lushbinary","url":"https://lushbinary.com/blog/gpt-5-6-sol-benchmarks-terminalbench-agentic-deep-dive/"},{"title":"GPT-5.5 Benchmarks, Pricing & Speed — July 2026 | BenchLM.ai","url":"https://benchlm.ai/models/gpt-5-5"},{"title":"Best AI Models: April + May 2026 Leaderboard (GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.7, DeepSeek V4)","url":"https://www.buildfastwithai.com/blogs/best-ai-models-may-2026-leaderboard"},{"title":"AI Leaderboard 2026: Compare & Rank 300+ Top AI Models by Intelligence, Speed & Price","url":"https://llm-stats.com/"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the DeepResearch Bench RACE leaderboard and the judge model transition:

Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, the DeepResearch Bench team benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset. All three candidates exceeded the human inter-annotator agreement baseline by 1.3–3 points, with GPT-5.5 winning on Overall, PAR, and FAS.

GPT-5.5 was adopted as the new RACE evaluator (with GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline). The previous Gemini-2.5-Pro / Gemini-2.5-Flash evaluation code is preserved on the Gemini-2.5 branch.

A dual-acceptance window runs now through May 31, 2026, accepting submissions evaluated under both the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and the new one (GPT-5.5), with results displayed on two separate leaderboards so rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: What judge/evaluator model is currently used for RACE and FACT? Is there any note about changing the judge model, deprecating Gemini-2.5-Pro, or a new leaderboard? Quote any dated changelog/announcement lines verbatim.

Quote verbatim the full paragraph of the ablation on hierarchical writing (section on writer ablation), and quote verbatim the THINK action description in the writer section. Also quote the Setup sentence naming the judge model.

content.content:
# Current Judge Models and Recent Changes

**Current evaluators:** GPT-5.5 (RACE) and GPT-5.4-mini (FACT)

**Key announcement from May 11, 2026:**

> "Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, we benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements"

The benchmark switched from Gemini-2.5-Pro to GPT-5.5 after testing multiple alternatives on 200 human-annotated tasks, where GPT-5.5 exceeded the human inter-annotator agreement baseline (68.78%) and won on overall performance metrics.

**Leaderboard transition period:** Submissions are accepted under both the legacy (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and new (GPT-5.5) evaluators through May 31, 2026, displayed on separate leaderboards. Full migration to GPT-5.5 begins June 1, 2026.

# Requested Quotes from WebWeaver Paper

**Ablation on Hierarchical Writing (Full Paragraph):**

"To empirically validate our hierarchical writing process, we conducted a critical ablation study comparing our hierarchical writer against a brute-force baseline that attempts to include the entire memory bank to generate the final report in multiple steps, which is similar to the workflow of LongWriter (Bai et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3#bib.bib4)). The results in Fig. [11](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3#S4.F11 "Figure 11 ‣ 4.3 Analysis ‣ 4 Experiments ‣ WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence withDynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research"), [11](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3#S4.F11 "Figure 11 ‣ 4.3 Analysis ‣ 4 Experiments ‣ WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence withDynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research") are unequivocal: our hierarchical approach dramatically outperforms the brute-force method across every metric, confirming that a hierarchical writing strategy is essential."

**THINK Action Description in Writer Section:**

"It then executes a targeted retrieval action, pulling only the relevant evidence from the memory bank as indicated by the outline's citations. With evidence, the writer enters a crucial reasoning phase with a think action. It analyzes the previous sections and retrieved content, synthesizes key insights, selects the most compelling pieces of evidence, and formulates a coherent narrative structure for the section."

**Setup Sentence Naming Judge Model:**

"We adopt Gemini-2.5-pro as the judgement model by following the benchmark."

input.command:
cd /tmp && curl -sL https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3 -o ww3.html && wc -c ww3.html && python3 - <<'EOF'
import re,html
t=open('/tmp/ww3.html',encoding='utf-8',errors='ignore').read()
x=re.sub(r'<[^>]+>',' ',t); x=html.unescape(x); x=re.sub(r'\s+',' ',x)
for kw in ['most striking','42.72','50.02','43.82','49.79','internal monologue','prune','Prune','brute-force','86.73','93.37','judgement model','LongWriter']:
    for m in re.finditer(re.escape(kw),x):
        print('###',kw,':',x[max(0,m.start()-450):m.start()+450],'\n')
EOF

### internal monologue : s write the first section.” It then executes a targeted retrieval action, pulling only the relevant evidence from the memory bank as indicated by the outline’s citations. With evidence, the writer enters a crucial reasoning phase with a think action. It analyzes the previous sections and retrieved content, synthesizes key insights, selects the most compelling pieces of evidence, and formulates a coherent narrative structure for the section. This internal monologue is critical for moving beyond simple summarization to genuine synthesis. Only after this internal analysis is formed does the writer proceed to the writing action, composing the prose and encapsulating it within “<write>” tags. Once a section is complete, its corresponding source materials are explicitly pruned from the context window and replaced with a placeholder message. This dynamic retrieval-and-pruning mechanism is the c 

### prune : nsights, selects the most compelling pieces of evidence, and formulates a coherent narrative structure for the section. This internal monologue is critical for moving beyond simple summarization to genuine synthesis. Only after this internal analysis is formed does the writer proceed to the writing action, composing the prose and encapsulating it within “<write>” tags. Once a section is complete, its corresponding source materials are explicitly pruned from the context window and replaced with a placeholder message. This dynamic retrieval-and-pruning mechanism is the cornerstone of our approach: it ensures the writer’s context remains highly relevant and coherent for the next cycle, mitigates context overflow, and prevents cross-sectional interference. This entire process repeats hierarchically for all sections until the writer outputs “<terminate>” token, signaling the completion of the 

### brute-force : cycle. It iteratively interleaves evidence acquisition from web searches with the continuous refinement and optimization of a report outline. The output of this exploratory phase is not just a collection of sources but a comprehensive, well-structured outline where each section is explicitly linked via citations to a curated memory bank of source evidence. Subsequently, the writer takes over for the synthesis phase. To circumvent the pitfalls of brute-force generation and noisy-context issues, the writer adopts a section-wise and memory-grounded synthesis approach. For each section of the outline, it performs targeted retrieval of only the pertinent evidence from the memory bank using cations and composes the content. This division of labor ensures that the final report is not only coherent and well-organized but also deeply source-grounded, faithfully mirroring the rigor of human-led de 

### brute-force : ce. This enhanced grounding and structure culminate in a plan that is itself more insightful (improving by 10-15 scores on both benchmarks). Therefore, this direct assessment confirms that our iterative planner is not just adding content but is actively forging a superior, more coherent, and better-supported blueprint—the foundational prerequisite for a high-quality final report. The context management superiority of our hierarchical writing process is quantitatively validated in Figure 12 . At each individual writing step, our method provides the model with a significantly smaller, more focused context compared to the brute-force baseline. This design dramatically reduces the model’s cognitive load. Freed from the burden of navigating a cluttered and noisy context, the writer can perform more thorough synthesis for each section. This leads to a richer output that cumulatively grows with each step, and as the plot clearly indicates, surpasses the baseline in total output tokens precisely at step 6, ultimately generating a substantially longer and more detailed  

### brute-force : tion to improve consistency (Yang et al., 2023 ; Wang et al., 2025 ; Huot et al., 2025 ) . More recently, agent-based frameworks have become a mainstream solution. Systems like LongWriter (Bai et al., 2025 ) , and CogWriter (Wan et al., 2025 ) employ a "plan-then-write" strategy, where a planner first creates an outline, and a writer then conditions on this plan to produce the full text. However, these methods rely on a static initial plan and a brute-force writing strategy by feeding all the evidence into LLMs. In contrast, our approach uniquely enables the outline to be dynamically optimized in tandem with the evidence acquisition process, allowing for a comprehensive, source-grounded research outline. While recent works (Huot et al., 2025 ; Shao et al., 2024 ) adopted the multi-agent paradigm to write sections in parallel, with evidence retrieval based on the section title, the separa

Both the planner and writer are embodiments of this principle: they use tools to dynamically explore, structure, and write, rather than passively processing it in a single pass. This work does not just present a better agent system; it presents a new blueprint for building the agent system that masters intensive knowledge through deliberate actions, not just brute-force attention. Furthermore, the accurate retrieved content using citations also brings higher citation accuracy (86.73 → 93.37) and supportiveness (90.95 → 98.73). In conclusion, these results provide definitive evidence that emulating the human cognitive process of focused, section-by-section writing is not merely a beneficial choice but a fundamental requirement for generating coherent, insightful, and reliable long-form reports. === main ===

DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents

DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents

input.command: cd /tmp && curl -s "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md" -o dr.md && grep -n -iE "gpt-5|gemini-2.5|judge|evaluator|deprecat|leaderboard|2026" dr.md | head -60

content.content: 7: 8: 16:+ [11 May 2026] 🎯 **Official Evaluator Switched to GPT-5.5**: Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, we benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = **68.78%**). All three candidates exceed this baseline by 1.3–3 points; **GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS**. We are adopting it as the new RACE evaluator (with **GPT-5.4-mini** for the FACT pipeline). Scores: 18: | Candidate evaluator | Overall ↑ | PAR | OPC | FAP | FAS | 20: | **GPT-5.5** 🥇 | **71.82** | **73.00** | 89.70 | 65.35 | **59.23** | 24:+ [11 May 2026] 📢 **Leaderboard Migration Plan**: 26: > - **Now – 31 May 2026 (dual-acceptance window)**: We accept submissions evaluated under **both** the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) **and** the new one (GPT-5.5). Results are displayed on **two separate leaderboards** so the rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator. 27: > - **By 1 June 2026 (full migration)**: For the results reported in the original DRB paper, we will re-evaluate them under GPT-5.5 and migrate them to the new leaderboard automatically. For prior community submissions evaluated under Gemini-2.5-Pro, if you would like to keep your entry on the new leaderboard, please contact us per [Submit to Leaderboard](#submit-to-leaderboard) and re-submit following the updated requirements. New submitters: follow the keys / config in [API Configuration](#api-configuration) below. After 1 June, Gemini-2.5-Pro acceptance ends and only the GPT-5.5 leaderboard is maintained going forward. 28: > - **GPT-5.5 leaderboard status**: still under construction — expected to launch within a week, alongside the migrated scores. 29: > - **Legacy code**: the previous Gemini-2.5-Pro / Gemini-2.5-Flash evaluation code is preserved on the [`Gemini-2.5`](https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/tree/Gemini-2.5) branch. 31:+ [11 May 2026] 🔧 **Evaluation Pipeline v2**: In the new release we have refined the article-cleaning logic, using a chunk-based strategy to better support very long articles. 33:+ [6 Feb 2026] 🚀 **DeepResearch Bench II Release**: We have released **DeepResearch Bench II (DRB II)** ([homepage](https://agentresearchlab.org/benchmarks/deepresearch-bench-ii/index.html#home)｜[repo](https://github.com/imlrz/DeepResearch-Bench-II)｜[paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.08536)). We welcome you to evaluate and exchange ideas. Note that DRB II, as a follow-up to DRB, has a different evaluation focus from DRB; **DRB will continue to be maintained and updated** after the release of DRB II. For more details, please refer to the [DRB II paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.08536). 35:+ [6 Feb 2026] 📚 **New Papers from Our Lab**: We welcome you to check out the new papers from our lab ([Agent Research Lab](https://agentresearchlab.org/index.html)): 44: **If you want to evaluate your deep research agent** please see the leaderboard submission requirements below and contact us at dumingxuan@mail.ustc.edu.cn and imlrz@mail.ustc.edu.cn. 46:+ [15 July 2025] ⚡️⚡️ **Major Update**: Added comprehensive evaluation of **Kimi-Researcher**, **Doubao-DeepResearch**, and **Claude-Researcher**. Upgraded evaluation infrastructure with **Gemini-2.5-Pro** for RACE and **Gemini-2.5-Flash** for FACT evaluation (since superseded — see top of News). All raw research articles and evaluation scores are now available on our [**Hugging Face Leaderboard**](https://huggingface.co/spaces/Ayanami0730/DeepResearch-Leaderboard) for comprehensive analysis and comparison. 116:**View Latest Leaderboard**: Visit our [**DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard**](https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard) for real-time updated evaluation results, detailed comparative analysis, and raw data. 118:### Submit to Leaderboard 120:If you would like to obtain an **official leaderboard entry** on DeepResearch Bench, please prepare the following materials and send them by email to: 127:1. **A temporary key with access to GPT-5.5** 194:| Backend | RACE judge (`Model`) | FACT judge (`FACT_Model`) | 196:| openrouter | `openai/gpt-5.5` | `openai/gpt-5.4-mini` | 197:| openai | `gpt-5.5` | `gpt-5.4-mini` |

content.text:
Verified against the primary source (arXiv HTML v3, fetched and grepped) and the benchmark's own repo.

**What checks out (verbatim, exactly as claimed):**
- The ablation paragraph in §4.3 of arXiv:2509.13312v3 reads: *"To empirically validate our hierarchical writing process, we conducted a critical ablation study comparing our hierarchical writer against a brute-force baseline that attempts to include the entire memory bank to generate the final report in multiple steps, which is similar to the workflow of LongWriter... The most striking improvements are in insight (42.72 → 50.02) and readability (43.82 → 49.79), which directly validates our hypothesis on attentional management; by focusing the model on a curated context for each section, it can perform deeper reasoning rather than shallow summarization. Furthermore, the accurate retrieved content using citations also brings higher citation accuracy (86.73 → 93.37) and supportiveness (90.95 → 98.73)."*
- The retrieve → think → write → prune loop is verbatim: *"...enters a crucial reasoning phase with a think action. It analyzes the previous sections and retrieved content, synthesizes key insights... This internal monologue is critical for moving beyond simple summarization to genuine synthesis... Once a section is complete, its corresponding source materials are explicitly pruned from the context window and replaced with a placeholder message."*
- Arithmetic: +7.30 insight, +5.97 readability. Correct.

**Why it is STALE, not CONFIRMED:**
- The paper's Setup says explicitly: *"We adopt Gemini-2.5-pro as the judgement model by following the benchmark."*
- The DeepResearch Bench repo README (primary source, `Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench`, main branch) carries a dated news entry: **"[11 May 2026] Official Evaluator Switched to GPT-5.5"** — RACE judge is now `gpt-5.5`, FACT judge `gpt-5.4-mini`; full migration 1 June 2026; the Gemini-2.5-Pro code is archived on a `Gemini-2.5` branch (branch list confirms `GPT-5.5` and `Gemini-2.5` branches exist). There is also a DeepResearch Bench II (Feb 2026).
- So 42.72 / 50.02 / 43.82 / 49.79 are legacy-judge numbers. They cannot be compared to any POLARIS score measured under the current evaluator, and "42.72 insight" is not a meaningful target on today's leaderboard.

**Two accuracy problems in the claim's framing (independent of staleness):**
1. "with retrieval held constant" — the paper never states this. It is a strong inference (both arms consume the same planner-built memory bank), but it is not an asserted controlled variable.
2. "MONOLITHIC composition" is a mischaracterization of the baseline. The paper's baseline generates *"in multiple steps"* (LongWriter-style) while keeping the entire memory bank in context. The variable under test is **per-section targeted retrieval + pruning**, not one-call-vs-many-calls. POLARIS's "12 monolithic 633-word paragraphs" only maps onto this baseline if POLARIS is also carrying all evidence in context for every section; if the failure is simply long undifferentiated paragraphs, this ablation is not evidence for the proposed fix.

Files: none in-repo were load-bearing; all verification was against `https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3` and `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md`.

input.reasoning:
Quote and numbers verified verbatim in the primary source (arXiv:2509.13312v3, §4.3): "The most striking improvements are in insight (42.72 -> 50.02) and readability (43.82 -> 49.79)... citation accuracy (86.73 -> 93.37) and supportiveness (90.95 -> 98.73)." The retrieve/think/write/prune mechanism is also verbatim, including "This internal monologue is critical for moving beyond simple summarization to genuine synthesis" and "its corresponding source materials are explicitly pruned from the context window and replaced with a placeholder message." Deltas (+7.30 insight, +5.97 readability) are arithmetically correct.

HOWEVER the paper's Setup states: "We adopt Gemini-2.5-pro as the judgement model by following the benchmark." The DeepResearch Bench repo (Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench, main README, dated news entry 11 May 2026) states the official RACE evaluator was switched to GPT-5.5 (FACT to GPT-5.4-mini) ahead of Google's 17 June 2026 Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecation, with full leaderboard migration on 1 June 2026 and the legacy Gemini pipeline archived on a `Gemini-2.5` branch. Branch listing confirms both `GPT-5.5` and `Gemini-2.5` branches. A successor benchmark (DRB II, Feb 2026) also exists. Therefore all four cited scores are legacy-judge numbers and are not comparable to any POLARIS score measured under the current evaluator; "42.72 insight" is not a valid target today.

Two additional framing errors, independent of staleness: (1) "with retrieval held constant" is never asserted by the paper -- it is a plausible inference (both arms draw on the same planner-built memory bank) but not a stated control. (2) "MONOLITHIC composition" misdescribes the baseline: the paper's brute-force arm writes "in multiple steps" (LongWriter-style) while keeping the ENTIRE memory bank in context. The tested variable is per-section targeted retrieval plus pruning, not single-call vs multi-call generation. So POLARIS's "12 monolithic 633-word paragraphs" only maps onto this baseline if POLARIS also carries all evidence in context for every section; if the actual failure is undifferentiated paragraph structure, this ablation does not support the proposed fix. The directional mechanism claim (section-wise writing with pruning beats full-context writing) is supported; the specific numbers as a POLARIS target are not.
input.corrected: The quoted ablation text and numbers are accurate to arXiv:2509.13312v3, but they were produced with Gemini-2.5-Pro as the RACE judge ("We adopt Gemini-2.5-pro as the judgement model by following the benchmark," §4.1). Per the benchmark's own repo (https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench, README news entry dated 11 May 2026), the official RACE evaluator is now GPT-5.5 (FACT: GPT-5.4-mini), with full leaderboard migration on 1 June 2026 and the Gemini-2.5-Pro pipeline preserved only on the legacy `Gemini-2.5` branch. WebWeaver's 42.72/50.02/43.82/49.79 have not (as far as I can confirm) been re-scored under GPT-5.5, so they are not comparable to a POLARIS run evaluated today. Also, the ablation baseline is not "monolithic": the paper describes it as generating "in multiple steps... similar to the workflow of LongWriter" while including the entire memory bank in context; the controlled variable is per-section targeted retrieval plus context pruning, and the paper never states that retrieval was held constant. Sources: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3 ; https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md
